coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of fluorescence sensor based on semi-covalent dummy molecularly imprinted silica on silane-modified carbon quantum dot for highly selective and sensitive detection of bisphenol A in spirits
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhiying
Sun, Xiaoli
Xu, Yalan
Yang, Luoxing
Wang, Muhua
Xia, Yueyi
Wang, Yan
Tang, Yu
Qiao, Chen
Lin, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Spirits, a Chinese liquor, is susceptible to bisphenol A (BPA) contamination during production and storage. Therefore, developing an accurate and sensitive method for BPA detection in spirits samples is necessary. In this study, a fluorescence sensor (Si-CQDs@DMIPs) with a low detection limit for BPA was synthesized by combining semi-covalent dummy molecular imprinting and silane-modified carbon quantum dot (Si-CQD) techniques. The structure, morphology, and fluorescence properties of Si-CQDs@DMIPs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS). The principle of the selective recognition and detection of BPA by the prepared Si-CQDs@DMIPs is that the molecular imprinting layer can selectively capture BPA and quench the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs, due to the electron transfer from BPA to the Si-CQDs. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs sensor showed a good linear decrease (R 2 > 0.99) when BPA concentration was 6–1800 nmol/L, and the detection limit was 0.65 nmol/L (3 σ/S). When applied to spirits samples, Si-CQDs@DMIPs exhibited recovery rates between 81.5% and 93.8% with relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 5) of 3.0–5.8% (spiked at 50, 200, 500, and 1000 nmol/L). Overall, Si-CQDs@DMIPs enable the highly selective and sensitive direct measurement and rapid screening of BPA in spirits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Dummy template
Semi-covalent imprinting
Fluorescence sensor
Spirits

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of the fluorescence sensor Si-CQDs@DMIPs.
(A) FTIR spectra of APTES and Si-CQDs; (B) XPS spectrum of Si-CQDs; (C) UV-Vis spectrum of Si-CQDs; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 340–365 nm.

(A) FTIR spectra 1: Si-CQDs@DMIPs, FTIR spectrum 2: Si-CQDs@NIPs; (B) Fluorescence spectra of Si-CQDs@DMIPs at different excitation wavelengths; (C) UV-Vis spectrum of Si-CQDs@DMIPs; (D) a: Si-CQDs@DMIPs after extraction of dummy template PP; b: Si-CQDs@DMIPs before extraction of PP; and c: Si-CQDs@NIPs.
(A) Effect of Si-CQDs@DMIPs dosage; (B) Effect of incubation time; and (C) Effect of pH.
(A) Stern–Volmer plot of BPA determined by Si-CQDs@DMIPs; (B) Stern–Volmer of BPA determined by Si-CQDs@NIPs; (C); (D) Standard curves of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs.
BPA analysis of the commercial spirits samples.using Si-CQDs@DMIPs and HPLC methods.
Comparison of the linear range and detection limit of our method with those reported in other literatures.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of fluorescence sensor based on semi-covalent dummy molecularly imprinted silica on silane-modified carbon quantum dot for highly selective and sensitive detection of bisphenol A in spirits

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Health, Zhejiang A&F University, Lin'an, Hangzhou 311300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Health, Zhejiang A&F University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lishui University, 1 Xueyuan Road, Lishui 323000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Lishui University, 1 Xueyuan Road, Lishui 323000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food and Health, Zhejiang A&F University, Lin'an, Hangzhou 311300, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Health, Zhejiang A&F University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spirits, a Chinese liquor, is susceptible to bisphenol A (BPA) contamination during production and storage. Therefore, developing an accurate and sensitive method for BPA detection in spirits samples is necessary. In this study, a fluorescence sensor (Si-CQDs@DMIPs) with a low detection limit for BPA was synthesized by combining semi-covalent dummy molecular imprinting and silane-modified carbon quantum dot (Si-CQD) techniques. The structure, morphology, and fluorescence properties of Si-CQDs@DMIPs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS). The principle of the selective recognition and detection of BPA by the prepared Si-CQDs@DMIPs is that the molecular imprinting layer can selectively capture BPA and quench the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs, due to the electron transfer from BPA to the Si-CQDs. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs sensor showed a good linear decrease (R 2 > 0.99) when BPA concentration was 6–1800 nmol/L, and the detection limit was 0.65 nmol/L (3 σ/S). When applied to spirits samples, Si-CQDs@DMIPs exhibited recovery rates between 81.5% and 93.8% with relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 5) of 3.0–5.8% (spiked at 50, 200, 500, and 1000 nmol/L). Overall, Si-CQDs@DMIPs enable the highly selective and sensitive direct measurement and rapid screening of BPA in spirits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA), known as diphenylolpropane, is extensively employed in the manufacturing of food contact materials (FCMs). BPA is a well-known endocrine-disrupting compound (EDC), which can stimulate or inhibit endogenous hormonal responses, thereby interfering with reproductive and developmental processes [1–3] BPA can adversely affect human health, even at sub-nanogram levels (i.e., 0.23 ng/mL) [4]. For example, Vilarinho [5] found that BPA can migrate from FCMs to food, particularly when eating utensils were heated, exposed to bases or acids, overused, and exposed to microwaves. The consumption of BPA-contaminated food has been identified as its primary source of human exposure [6].

Currently, the presence of BPA in food and its detrimental effects on human health are of great concern to regulators and the public. Therefore, accurate and sensitive measurement techniques for determination of BPA in food samples are urgently needed. At present, instrumental analysis techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7] and high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [8] were mostly used. However, these instruments are costly, involve complex pre-treatment procedures, demand specialized operations, and are time-consuming, making them unsuitable for simple and rapid analysis. In contrast, fluorescence detection based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) provides a cost-effective, highly sensitive and water compatibility approach [9] for BPA detection. For instance, Eunbi Hwang [10]synthesised a carbon quantum dot (CQD) using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as a precursor by a hydrothermal synthesis method and was used for detection of bisphenol A in aqueous solutions.

Recently, a new type of silane-modified CQDs (Si-CQDs) has attracted much attention due to its improved fluorescence characteristics, convenient surface functionalization and excellent dispersion properties[11–13]. The element Si is adjacent to C in the periodic table and has similar atomic structure and good bio-compatibility, provides a broad direction for the doping of CQDs, which means that the physicochemical properties of CQDs can be effectively modulated, helps prevent the accumulation. Doping of silicon can increase the high yield of electrons and thus fluorescence intensity[14].Liang et al. [11]. prepared Si-CQDs using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane as the carbon and silicon source, and used it as a fluorescent probe for dual-readout detection of berberine hydrochloride with a limit of detection (LOD) of 50 nmol/L. Liu et al. [15]. prepared Si-CQDs through hydrothermal treatment of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine, and applied to the determination of hydroquinone (HQ) with a 77 nmol/L detection limit. However, the reports about the synthesis of Si-CQDs and its application in fluorescence sensing are still very limited. In addition, although Si-CQDs shows promising applications in the field of fluorescence sensing and detection, there are still limitations in selective and sensitive detection, which hinders their application in real sample analysis [16].

Molecular imprinting technology simulates antigen–antibody recognition to construct molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific binding sites [17]. Combining the high selectivity of molecular imprinting with the excellent fluorescence response properties of Si-CQDs enables the construction of high-performance molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors[18]. In addition, the selective enrichment of target compounds by MIPs is expected to improve the detection sensitivity of fluorescence sensing[19] Liu et al.[20] . used a non-covalent approach to develop carbon dot-conjugated molecularly imprinted polymers to selectively determine BPA in water with a detection limit of 1.5 nmol L-1.

However, template leakage and the low effect of common non-covalent imprinting in strongly polar solvent systems are the main problems that limit the application of molecular imprinting techniques in fluorescence sensing. To address the template leakage problem, the dummy template imprinting technique can be employed, since the bleeding of structural analogs does not interfere with the determination of the target analyte [21]. In our previous works, dummy templates such as phenolphthalein (PP) and 1,1,1-tris(4-hydroxyphenyl)ethane (THPE) were selected for high selective imprinting of bisphenols, and were used in the sample preparation or adsorption removal of bisphenols[22,23]. Semi-covalent imprinting is a combination of covalent and non-covalent methods [24], which combines the advantages of the two techniques. The template-monomer interaction during the polymerization is highly stable, and the binding of target molecular is fast during the recognition process [25]. Most of all, it can avoid the interference of acidity or alkalinity on the template-monomer interaction during the sol-gel process, compare with the common non-covalent method. To date, semi-covalent imprinting for constructing fluorescent nanosensors specifically for the detection of bisphenols have not been reported.

Herein, we used phenolphthalein (PP), which is a green, non-toxic, and inexpensive compound, as the dummy template, along with 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (ICPTES) as the functional monomer, to synthesize a dummy template-monomer complex (PP-ICPTES). The fluorescence sensor (Si-CQDs@DMIPs) was then achieved using semi-covalent imprinting and the sol–gel method in the presence of Si-CQDs. The structure，morphology, and fluorescence properties of Si-CQDs@DMIPs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS). This approach enabled the development of a simple, sensitive, and highly reproducible fluorescent detection technique for selectively and rapidly identifying BPA using a Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescence sensor. Parameters, including pH, concentration, and response time, were optimized, and the selectivity, accuracy, and reproducibility of the method were systematically investigated.

Citric acid (CA), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), phenolphthalein (PP) and 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (ICPTES) were purchased from Shanghai，C Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. The ammonia solution and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Hangzhou Shuanglin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. BPA, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), barium chloride (BaCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) were procured from Beijing J&K Scientific Ltd. Bisphenol B (BPB) and Bisphenol AP (BPAP) were purchased from TCI Chemicals, Shanghai, China.

Surface morphology was analyzed using TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, Facility Establishment Identifier, USA). The Nicolet 380 (Thermo Scientific, USA) instrument was used for FTIR analysis. Furthermore, a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax-4c, Horiba, Japan) was used to record fluorescence measurements. XPS was performed using the Axis Ultra DLD instrument (Kratos, UK). UV-Vis spectrophotometry was performed using the UV-1900i instrument (Shimadzu, Japan).

Si-CQDs were created using the previously published process, but with a few changes [26]. Briefly, 0.5 g CA was dissolved in 10.0 mL APTES with ultrasound for 10 min. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted in 210 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain Si-CQDs with high-quantum yield. The Si-CQDs were then dispersed in an anhydrous ethanol solution at a v/v ratio of 1:1 and stored at 4 ℃.

PP-ICPTES was synthesized based on the previously reported method by our team [23]. PP, with the same symmetrical hydroxyphenyl structure as BPA, was applied as the dummy template, and ICPTES was used as the semi-covalent functional monomer. First, 10 mmol PP was dissolved in 15.0 mL anhydrous DMF, and 20 mmol ICPTES was added dropwise with continuous stirring at 25 ℃. The resulting mixture was reacted at 80 ℃ under a nitrogen atmosphere for 48 h. Once the reaction was complete ( Fig. 1), DMF was removed using vacuum distillation to obtain a pale-yellow, viscousproduct of PP-ICPTES. PP-ICPTES was sealed and stored under a nitrogen atmosphere.

Sol-gel synthesis was employed to create Si-CQDs@DMIPs. First, 20.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol were used to dissolve 1 mmol of PP-ICPTES with the aid of an ultrasonicator. Then, 200 L of a Si-CQD solution was added dropwise while being vigorously mechanically stirred. Next, 8 mmol of TEOS and 1.0 mL of ammonia solution were added sequentially, and remain reaction at room temperature for three hours. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes. The obtained Si-CQDs@DMIPs sample was cleaned and dried before being refluxed for three hours at 180 °C in a DMSO-water (v/v = 5:1) solution to remove PP dummy template. The Si-CQDs@DMIPs were finally heated under vacuum for 6 hours. For comparison, non-imprinted nanogels (Si-CQDs@NIPs) were prepared using the same protocol and washing procedures, except for replacing PP-ICPTES with 2 mmol of APTES.

The excitation and emission slit width is set to 5 nm, and the excitation wavelength was 365 nm, for all measurements of fluorescence intensity., and emission wavelength measurement range was 380–650 nm. The synthesized Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs were thoroughly dispersed in anhydrous ethanol using ultrasonic vibration. Equal volumes of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs dispersions were added to 3-mL cuvettes, and then BPA solutions of different concentrations were added. The fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs was measured after incubation for 2 min.

Addition of different concentrations of bisphenol A changes the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs dispersions, and the fluorescence burst amplitude (F/F 0 − 1) increased linearly with the raise of BPA concentration. Fluorescence quenching can be quantitatively accounted by the Stern–Volmer equation: F/F 0 − 1 = Ksv × C BPA [27], where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of BPA, respectively, C BPA is the concentration of BPA, and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer constant.

In our previous studies, the class-selectivity of BPA, PP and 1,1,1-tris(4-hydroxyphenyl)ethane (THPE) template towards BPs were systematic investigation, and the results showed that THPE had better selectivity for bisphenols in polymer imprinting systems[22], whereas PP had better selectivity for bisphenols in silicon-based imprinting systems[23]. The adsorption isotherms of BPA using different semi-covalent dummy template was shown in Fig. S1. THPE has three p-benzene hydroxyl groups, and the imprinting site is likely too tight and difficult to access in the silicon-based imprinting system, resulting in an unsatisfactory outcome. Therefore, in this work, we selected PP as a dummy template for BPA.The dummy template-monomer complex PP-ICPTES was synthesized according to a previous method and applied to the preparation of a semi-covalent dummy molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor of BPA for the first time.

Si-CQDs@DMIPs were synthesized by a sol-gel process, as can be seen in Fig. 2. Firstly, Si-CQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method.Within this process, anhydrous CA was carbonized at high temperatures, and the amino group on APTES underwent acylation with the carboxyl group of anhydrous CA, which resulted in the formation of Si-CQDs exhibiting PL. Subsequently, under the catalysis of ammonia, PP-ICPTES, ICPTES, and TEOS were hydrolytically cross-linked to form a gel coating on the surface of Si-CQDs. This coating effectively embedded the Si-CQDs into the binding sites, thereby facilitating the successful creation of Si-CQDs@DMIPs. Template molecules were removed using solvent reflux. The Si-CQDs@DMIPs possessing specific recognition capabilities for BPA were then obtained.

The morphology and lattice of Si-CQDs were characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 3, the prepared Si-CQDs are spherical with an average diameter of about 5 nm. Further analysis revealed that Si-CQDs exhibited obvious lattice fringes under TEM, and the fringe spacing was approximately 0.15 nm, indicating that Si-CQDs are carbon quantum dots with high crystallinity[28]. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. S1 exhibits two obvious peaks at 10° and 22°, which further confirmed the existence of graphitic carbon (001) and (002) crystal planes in Si-CQDs[29,30].

The functional groups and elemental compositions of Si-CQDs were characterized using FTIR and XPS analyses. Fig. 4 A shows that the FTIR spectra of Si-CQDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks compared to that for APTES. The peak at 1670 cm−1 was due to the CO stretching of the secondary amide carbonyl group, whereas the peak at 1570 cm−1 represented the deformation vibration of R–NH–R. These two distinct absorption peaks indicated the formation of the CO–NH group, which confirmed the acylation of the CQDs with APTES at high temperatures, and suggested the successful synthesis of Si-CQDs. Furthermore, the XPS spectrum of Si-CQDs in Fig. 4B shows four distinct peaks at 102.08 (Si 2p), 285.08 (C 1 s), 399.08 (N 1 s), and 532.08 (O 1 s) eV. As shown in Fig. S2A, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three-unit moieties: CC/C-C (284.45 eV)[31], C-O/C-N (285.25 eV), and CO (288.12 eV) [32]. The N 1 s spectrum can be resolved into two peaks at 398.94 eV and 400.3 eV, which are ascribed to pyridinic N and graphite, respectively (Fig. S2B)[33]. The O 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. S2C confirmed the existence of CO (531.98 eV), and C-O (530.88 eV) [34] in the Si-CQDs. In particular, the XPS Si 2p spectrum comprises one peaks at 102.33 eV[35], which can be assigned to Si-O (Fig. S2D). This result indicates that Si-CQDs primarily comprised C, N, Si, and O, which is consistent with the functional group characterization results of FTIR; therefore, the quantum dots are successfully silane functionalized [26].

The chemiluminescence properties of Si-CQDs are characterized using UV-Vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the peak at 225 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, and the peak at 354 nm is caused by the adsorption of the n-π* transition. These transitions may generate emission traps and excited surface states, which further enhance the fluorescence emission. Fig. 4D shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 340–365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs varies with the excitation wavelength. The excitation dependence is a remarkable feature of Si-CQDs, which further confirms its successful synthesis [36]. The maximum emission of Si-CQDs is located around 450 nm with a quantum yield of 8.18%. Because the maximum emission was achieved at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, this wavelength was chosen for subsequent experiments.

Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescence sensors were prepared by sol-gel method. Si-CQDs, PP-ICPTES, and TEOS. The morphology and structure of the product are shown in Fig. 5. Si-CQDs@DMIPs presented as an irregular nanogel form with a particle size of approximately 50 nm. As abovementioned, the average size of the Si-CQDs was approximately 5 nm. The increase in the size of nanocomposites may have been due to the coating of Si-CQDs by the imprinting layer during the hydrolytic crosslinking process, which was further verified by subsequent FTIR and fluorescence characterization.

The FTIR spectra of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs were shown in Fig. 6 A. The bands at 468 and 791 cm-1 are associated with O-Si-O and Si-O vibrations, respectively. The adsorption at 1080 cm-1 is also related to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si. These results indicated that the Si-CQDs are coated with an imprinted silica layer.The similarity of the main bands of the FTIR spectra of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs suggests that the two nanocomposites have similar chemical structures. In addition, the removal of the pseudotemplate PP from Si-CQDs@DMIPs was confirmed by water washing. These results further demonstrate the successful synthesis of Si-CQDs@DMIPs nanocomposites.

The Photoluminescence (PL) properties of Si-CQDs@DMIPs were studied using fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 6B shows that the emission of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was dependent on the excitation wavelength. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, and the emission wavelength also varied. The fluorescence intensity is highest at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. However, a further increase in excitation wavelength resulted in a gradual reduction in fluorescence intensity. In addition, according to the UV-Vis spectrum of Si-CQDs@DMIPs in Fig. 6 C, no obvious absorption peak between 200 and 600 nm is observed, indicating that the dummy template molecule (PP) has been successfully removed.

After removing of the dummy template, the fluorescence signal of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was significantly enhanced, while the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@NIPs remained weak (Fig. 6D). After further addition of bisphenol A, the fluorescence of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was obviously quenched. This phenomenon was attributed to the charge transfer between Si-CQDs@DMIPs and bisphenol A molecules [37]. In this recognition system, Si-CQDs@DMIPs and BPA molecules function as electron acceptors and donors, respectively.

To obtain the optimal conditions for the quantitative detection of BPA using the Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescence sensor, parameters including dosage of Si-CQDs@DMIPs, response time, and solution pH were adjusted.

To investigate the effect of different Si-CQDs@DMIPs dosages on the detection of BPA, samples of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg were dispesed in 100 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Subsequently, the same amount of BPA was added, and the fluorescence quenching ratio was measured as F 0/F – 1. Fig. 7 A shows that the quenching effect was most significant when the sample dosage was 40 mg, while the fluorescence spectrophotometer exceeded its maximum detection capacity at 50 mg/100 mL, resulting in inaccurate results. Therefore, the dosage of 40 mg/100 mL of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was selected for the subsequent condition optimization.

The incubation time represents the time to reach the maximum fluorescence quenching after the addition of BPA, and is an important parameter for BPA measurement. In this work, the fluorescence intensity was measured every 1 min for a total of 20 min, and the results are shown in Fig. 7B. The response of Si-CQDs@DMIPs remained stabilized after 2 min. During the experiment, the interaction between BPA and the Si-CQDs@DMIPs cavities increased, resulting in further quenching after 13 min. Therefore, to obtain quick and stable test results, 2 min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

Acidity and alkalinity will affect the binding of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and bisphenol A, and thus affect its fluorescence quenching efficiency. We investigated the fluorescence intensity changes of Si-CQDs@DMIPs in the pH range of 3–10 (Fig. 7 C) and found that the fluorescence quenching rate was highest at pH 6. In contrast, fluorescence quenching was weakened in alkaline environments due to the breakdown of the silica shell [38]. Therefore, pH 6 was selected as the optimal testing value.

The fluorescence quantitative detection performance of Si-CQDs@DMIPs for bisphenol A was evaluated under optimized conditions. The effects of different concentrations of BPA on the fluorescence quenching of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs are shown in Fig. 8 A and 8B, respectively. When the concentration of BPA was increased from 6 to 1800 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs decreased. However, the decline of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was more obvious than that of Si-CQDs@NIPs. This is primarily due to the molecular imprinting effect, which renders it more efficient for the enrichment of BPA, resulting in a higher detection sensitivity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs.

Further analysis showed that for Si-CQDs@DMIPs, when BPA concentration increased from 6 to 1800 nmol/L, two linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity and BPA concentration are obtained, as shown in Fig. 8 C. In the range of 6–150 nmol/L, the linear equation is y = 0.0199 x + 0.00121 (R 2 = 0.9966), and in the range of 150–1800 nmol/L, the linear relationship is y = 0.00588 x + 0.1648 (R 2 = 0.9965). At low concentrations, the fluorescence response was more sensitive and the slope was higher, which may be caused by the heterogeneous binding sites [39] of the molecularly imprinted materials. Moreover, the adsorption affinity for BPA was higher at low concentrations. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3 σ/k, where σ is the the standard deviation of the blank samples, and k is the slope of the standard curve. The LOD of Si-CQDs@DMIPs was 0.65 nmol/L, which is considerably lower than those reported in
Table 2.

For Si-CQDs@NIPs, when the concentration of BPA was changed from 100 to 1800 nmol/L, the linear equation was y = 0.0115 x + 0.1015 (R
2 = 0.9962), with an LOD of 1.77 nmol/L. The detection sensitivity and linear range of Si-CQDs@DMIPs were significantly superior to those of Si-CQDs@NIPs, further confirming the successful construction of the imprinted recognition sites.

Selectivity is an important evaluation criterion for assessing the measurement capability of Si-CQDs@DMIPs, because it determines the degree of interference from other components in real samples. In this study, various metal ions including Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, K+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Mg2+ and Cu2+, different organic compounds including bisphenol B (BPB), bisphenol AP (BPAP), dioctyl phthalate (DOP), sorbic alchol (SA), lactate (LA), and sunset yellow (SY) were used to test the selectivity of Si-CQDs@DMIPs. The selectivity of BPA detection was evaluated based on the value of F/F 0 − 1 at a concentration of 0.05 g/L for each interfering substance. The imprinting factor, calculated as the ratio of F/F 0 − 1 between Si-CQDs@DMIPs and Si-CQDs@NIPs, was 3.4, indicating significant imprinting selectivity of BPA. Fig. 9 show that the presence of interfering substances has minimal effect on the detection capability of Si-CQDs@DMIPs for BPA, demonstrating that the sensor can be used for direct, rapid, and selective determination of BPA in complex samples.

The recovery rates of the Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescent sensor were evaluated using commercial spirits samples spiked with 50, 200, 500, and 1000 nmol/L BPA, and the data are listed in Table 1. The results showed satisfactory BPA recovery rates ranging from 81.5% to 93.8%, with relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 5) of 2.6−3.3%. Detailed inter-day and intra-day precision are shown in Table S2. The reliability of this method was further verified by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with similar recovery rates (72.9–93.5%) as shown in Table 1. The storage stability of Si-CQDs@MIPs was also investigated, the quantitative precision can be maintained at 3.0–5.8% for three months. Furthermore, the constructed Si-CQDs@DMIPs had a lower LOD and larger dynamic range than the reported BPA sensors (Table 2). These findings highlight the advantages of employing a dummy template semi-covalent approach for synthesizing a BPA fluorescence sensor, which not only improves the selectivity and sensitivity, but also prevents the interference from other substances during the detection process. Therefore, the accuracy and reliability of the experimental results were improved.

In this study, a novel Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescence sensor was developed using the sol−gel method to determine the quantity of BPA in spirits samples. The sensor was prepared by coating a semi-covalent dummy molecularly imprinted silica on silane-modified CQDs. The principle is based on the synergistic effect of the selective enrichment of BPA by the imprinting layer and the fluorescence response of Si-CQDs to BPA. Compared with other reported BPA sensors, the constructed Si-CQDs@DMIPs fluorescence sensor has a wider dynamic range (6−1800 nmol/L) and lower LOD (0.65 nmol/L), indicating that the enrichment effect of the imprinting layer significantly enhances the detection efficiency of BPA in Si-CQDs. In addition, the fluorescence stability, response time, interference, and selectivity experiments showed that Si-CQDs@DMIPs had excellent selective quantitative detection performance for BPA. Furthermore, the real sample recovery experiment further verified the practicability of Si-CQDs@DMIPs. This study highlights the potential of using semi-covalent dummy molecular imprinting and silane functionalized quantum dot technologies in the detection of BPA in liquor, which opens a new approach in this field.

Chen Qiao : Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Luoxing Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Yi Lin: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhiying Wang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yalan Xu: Writing – review & editing. Xiaoli Sun: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yueyi Xia: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yan Wang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yu Tang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Muhua Wang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22076072, 22306082) and Fundamental Research Funds of Lishui Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2023GYX46).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134292.
